question 153.question: How do you calculate TMDA efficiency in the case of one carrier per link?
question 153.option_1: In the case of a ‘one carrier per link’ technique, P = N(N+1)/2, accounting for all possible directional carrier assignments.
question 153.option_2: In the case of a ‘one carrier per link’ technique, P = N, because each station communicates using a single shared carrier frequency.
question 153.option_3: In the case of a ‘one carrier per link’ technique, P= N(N-1).
question 153.option_4: In the case of a ‘one carrier per link’ technique, P = N-1, because each station communicates using a single shared carrier frequency.
question 153.option_5: In the case of a ‘one carrier per link’ technique, P = N+4, because each station communicates using a single shared carrier frequency.

question 155.question: How do you represent link margin?
question 155.option_1:
Link margin is simply the di erence in dB between the actual CNR and
 the required CNR: M= EIRP-FSL-L+\left[ \frac{G_{r}}{TS}\right]_{dB}+228.6-10log(B)-\left[ \frac{C}{N} \right]_{req.dB}
question 155.option_2:
Link margin is simply the di erence in dB between the actual CNR and
 the required CNR: M=L+\left[ \frac{G_{r}}{TS}\right]_{dB}+228.6-10log(B)-\left[ \frac{C}{N} \right]_{req.dB}
question 155.option_3:
Link margin is simply the di erence in dB between the actual CNR and
 the required CNR: M= EIRP-FSL-L +228.6-10log(B)-\left[ \frac{C}{N} \right]_{req.dB}
question 155.option_4:
Link margin is simply the di erence in dB between the actual CNR and
 the required CNR: M= EIRP-FSL-L+\left[ \frac{G_{r}}{TS}\right]_{dB}-\left[ \frac{C}{N} \right]_{req.dB}
question 155.option_5:
Link margin is simply the di erence in dB between the actual CNR and
 the required CNR: M= EIRP-228.6-10log(B)-\left[ \frac{C}{N} \right]_{req.dB}

question 156.question: How do you represent noise power at the output of a network?
question 156.option_1: N = αN₁ + N₂ , where N1 is the input noise, N2 is the noise added by the network, and α is the power gain.
question 156.option_2: N = N₁ + αN₂, where the input noise is amplified after the network adds its own noise.
question 156.option_3: N = α(N₁ + N₂), implying both input and internal noise are equally amplified.
question 156.option_4: N = N₁ + N₂ / α, suggesting internal noise decreases with gain.

question 157.question: How do you represent received signal under additive white Gauss noise?
question 157.option_2: It is given by  r=\pm B_{p}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma}}

question 158.question: How do you represent the Friis formula?
question 158.option_1: Friis formula can be written as C=EIRP(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r})^{2}\frac{G_{r}}{l}
question 158.option_2: Friis formula can be written as C=EIRP \frac{G_{r}}{l}
question 158.option_3: Friis formula can be written as C=(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r})^{2}\frac{G_{r}}{l}
question 158.option_4: Friis formula can be written as C=EIRP(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi r})^{2}

question 159.question: How do you represent the interelement time delay in a beam?
question 159.option_1: The interelement time delay is given by  \Delta t= -{\frac{d}{c}}cos\phi_{0}
question 159.option_2: The interelement time delay is given by  \Delta t= -{\frac{d}{c}}cos2\pi
question 159.option_3: The interelement time delay is given by  \Delta t= -{\frac{4d}{r}}cos\phi_{0}

question 160.question: How does one find the required bandwidth?
question 160.option_1: Required Bandwidth (BW) = Symbol Rate × (1 + Roll-Off Factor)
question 160.option_2: Symbol Rate (SR) = Data Rate / (Modulation Efficiency × FEC Rate)
question 160.option_4: Required Bandwidth (BW) = Symbol Rate × (1 - FEC factor)
question 160.option_5: Required Bandwidth (BW) = Modulation efficiency × (1 + Roll-Off Factor)

question 161.question: How is the doppler shift formula represented?
question 161.option_1: Doppler shift is given by  \Delta f=\frac{v_{r}}{c}f_{0}
question 161.option_2: Doppler shift is given by  \Delta f=\frac{T_{0}}{c}f_{0}
question 161.option_3: Doppler shift is given by  \beta f=\frac{v_{r}}{c}f_{0}
question 161.option_4: Doppler shift is given by  \phi f=\frac{v_{r}}{c}f_{0}
question 161.option_5: Doppler shift is given by  \Delta f=\frac{v_{r}}{c}f_{0}

question 162.question: How is the power flux density at the satellite situated at earth station antenna boresight calculated?
question 162.option_1: The power flux density is given by {\Phi}_{max}={P}_{T}{G}_{Tmax}/{4{\pi}R^{2}}
question 162.option_2: The power flux density is given by {\Omega}_{max}={P}_{T}{G}_{Tmax}/{4{\pi}R^{2}}
question 162.option_3: The power flux density is given by {\lambda}_{max}={P}_{T}{G}_{Tmax}/{4{\pi}R^{2}}
question 162.option_4: The power flux density is given by {\Phi}_{max}={R}_{T}{G}_{Tmax}/{4{\pi}R^{3}}

question 163.question: How is the signal-to-noise ratio calculated?
question 163.option_1: Signal to noise ratio is given by SNR=S/N
question 163.option_2: SNR = N / S, representing the ratio of noise to signal power.
question 163.option_3: SNR = S × N, indicating the product of signal and noise power.
question 163.option_4: SNR = (S + N) / 2, the average of signal and noise power levels.
question 163.option_5: SNR = S – N, representing the difference between signal and noise power.

question 165.question: How will you describe Friis equation in wireless communication?
question 165.option_1: Friis equation is given by [Pr = Pt * Gt * Gr * ({\lambda} / 4 * {\pi} * d)^{2}]
question 165.option_2: Friis equation is given by [Pr = Pt * Lx * Gr * ({\lambda} / 4 * {\pi} * d)^{4}]
question 165.option_3: Friis equation is given by [Pr = Pt * Gt * Gr * ({\lambda} / 2 * {\pi} * d)^{3}]

It is the link between the satellite and Earth station is governed by the basic microwave
